section.title: The Sin Offering
section.sectionRationale: The fourth of the five great offerings, the sin offering deals specifically with unintentional sin, scaled to the sinner's position, priest, whole congregation, leader, or common person. It continues the sacrificial law collection, showing that atonement is provided for sin committed even without deliberate intent.
section.thesis: The sin offering provides atonement for unintentional sin according to who has sinned, the anointed priest, the whole congregation, a leader, or an ordinary Israelite, teaching that sin's seriousness and its remedy are measured by the sinner's position and knowledge, yet forgiveness is available to every rank.
section.frameNote: Four movements, one for each category of sinner: the anointed priest, whose sin brings guilt on the whole people; the whole congregation acting in ignorance; the leader; and the common person, each concluding in forgiveness.

units.label: When the anointed priest sins
units.note: guilt spreads to the whole people

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Tell the Israelites to do as follows with one who sins {{shegagah|unintentionally}} against any of the LORD's commandments and does what is forbidden by them:
If the anointed priest sins, bringing guilt on the people, he must bring to the LORD a young bull without blemish as a {{chattat|sin offering}} for the sin he has committed.
He must bring the bull to the entrance to the Tent of Meeting before the LORD, lay his hand on the bull's head, and slaughter it before the LORD.
Then the anointed priest shall take some of the bull's blood and bring it into the Tent of Meeting.
The priest is to dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle some of it seven times before the LORD, in front of the veil of the sanctuary.

The priest must then put some of the blood on the horns of the altar of fragrant incense that is before the LORD in the Tent of Meeting. And he is to pour out the rest of the bull's blood at the base of the altar of burnt offering at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
Then he shall remove all the fat from the bull of the sin offering: the fat that covers the entrails, all the fat that is on them,
both kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver, which he is to remove with the kidneys,
just as the fat is removed from the ox of the peace offering. Then the priest shall burn them on the altar of burnt offering.
But the hide of the bull and all its flesh, with its head and legs and its entrails and dung,
all the rest of the bull, he must take outside the camp to a ceremonially clean place where the ashes are poured out, and there he must burn it on a wood fire on the ash heap.

units.label: When the whole congregation strays
units.note: the elders lay hands on the bull

verses.text: Now if the whole congregation of Israel strays unintentionally and the matter escapes the notice of the assembly so that they violate any of the LORD's commandments and incur guilt by doing what is forbidden,
when they become aware of the sin they have committed, then the assembly must bring a young bull as a sin offering and present it before the Tent of Meeting.
The elders of the congregation are to lay their hands on the bull's head before the LORD, and it shall be slaughtered before the LORD.
Then the anointed priest is to bring some of the bull's blood into the Tent of Meeting,
and he is to dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle it seven times before the LORD in front of the veil.

He is also to put some of the blood on the horns of the altar that is before the LORD in the Tent of Meeting, and he must pour out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar of burnt offering at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
And he is to remove all the fat from it and burn it on the altar.
He shall offer this bull just as he did the bull for the sin offering; in this way the priest will {{kipper|make atonement}} on their behalf, and they will be forgiven.
Then he is to take the bull outside the camp and burn it, just as he burned the first bull. It is the sin offering for the assembly.
When a leader sins unintentionally and does what is prohibited by any of the commandments of the LORD his God, he incurs guilt.
When he becomes aware of the sin he has committed, he must bring an unblemished male goat as his offering.
He is to lay his hand on the head of the goat and slaughter it at the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered before the LORD. It is a sin offering.
Then the priest is to take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger, put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and pour out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar.
He must burn all its fat on the altar, like the fat of the peace offerings; thus the priest will make atonement for that man's sin, and he will be forgiven.
And if one of the common people sins unintentionally and does what is prohibited by any of the LORD's commandments, he incurs guilt.
When he becomes aware of the sin he has committed, he must bring an unblemished female goat as his offering for that sin.
He is to lay his hand on the head of the sin offering and slaughter it at the place of the burnt offering.
Then the priest is to take some of its blood with his finger, put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and pour out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar.

Then he is to remove all the fat, just as it is removed from the peace offering, and the priest is to burn it on the altar as a pleasing aroma to the LORD. In this way the priest will make atonement for him, and he will be forgiven.
If, however, he brings a lamb as a sin offering, he must bring an unblemished female.
And he is to lay his hand on the head of the sin offering and slaughter it as a sin offering at the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered.
Then the priest is to take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger, put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and pour out the rest of its blood at the base of the altar.

And he shall remove all the fat, just as the fat of the lamb is removed from the peace offerings, and he shall burn it on the altar along with the offerings made by fire to the LORD. In this way the priest will make atonement for him for the sin he has committed, and he will be forgiven.

shegagah.gloss: An unintentional sin, an error or straying done without deliberate intent to rebel. This chapter provides atonement precisely for such sins, showing that even unplanned wrongdoing creates real guilt requiring a remedy.

chattat.gloss: The sin offering, from the same root as the word for sin itself. This sacrifice deals directly with the defiling effect of sin on the sanctuary and the sinner's standing before God, unlike the burnt or peace offerings which are more general acts of worship.

kipper.gloss: To atone, to cover. Repeated at the end of every case in this chapter, kaphar describes the priest's mediating action that results, each time, in the sinner being forgiven, nasla, the guilt fully removed.

basic.context: The sin offering, the fourth of the five great sacrifices, deals specifically with sin committed unintentionally, by mistake or ignorance rather than open defiance. The chapter walks through four cases in descending order of impact: the anointed priest, whose sin taints the whole people; the entire congregation; a leader; and finally an ordinary Israelite, each requiring a different animal and each ending in forgiveness.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes Israel's actual old covenant sacrificial system for dealing with unintentional sin; it is not a pattern of animal sacrifice for Christians today. Its abiding truth is that even unintended sin is real sin requiring atonement, and that God has always provided a way for guilty people, whoever they are, to be forgiven.
basic.originalLanguages: The Hebrew shegagah describes an unintentional sin, an error rather than deliberate rebellion, yet it still requires chattat, a sin offering, from the same root as sin itself. Every case in the chapter ends the same way, the priest makes kaphar, atonement, and the sinner is forgiven, nasla, a word meaning the guilt is fully lifted away.
basic.typology: The bull burned entirely outside the camp for the priest's and congregation's sin, its blood taken into the sanctuary itself, foreshadows Christ, who suffered outside the city gate to sanctify His people through His own blood, Hebrews 13:11-12. The sliding scale of offerings by rank pictures a God who deals seriously with every level of sin, from the highest office to the ordinary believer.
basic.christ: Hebrews draws directly on this chapter's pattern: bodies of animals whose blood is brought into the sanctuary are burned outside the camp, and so Jesus also suffered outside the gate to sanctify the people through His own blood, Hebrews 13:11-12. Christ is both the greater priest who mediates atonement and the greater sacrifice whose blood actually cleanses rather than merely covering sin until it is dealt with again.
basic.god: God takes unintentional sin seriously; ignorance of wrongdoing does not remove its guilt or its need for atonement. Yet He also graduates the offering according to what each sinner can bring and their position of responsibility, showing both His justice, which holds leaders to greater account, and His mercy, which forgives every rank fully and completely.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 13:11-12 links the sin offering burned outside the camp to Christ's suffering outside the gate. 1 John 1:9 promises that if we confess our sins, God is faithful and just to forgive us. Psalm 19:12 prays for cleansing from hidden faults, unintentional sins we may not even recognize.
basic.oneStory: The sin offering teaches that unintentional sin still creates real guilt and requires real atonement, whether committed by a priest, the whole nation, a leader, or an ordinary person. This anticipates Christ, whose blood, brought once into the true sanctuary, actually cleanses the conscience of sin, including sins we commit without even realizing it.

context.literary: This chapter's structure, moving from priest to congregation to leader to common person, mirrors the order of responsibility in Israel's society, with the animal required, and the ritual detail, decreasing as the case moves down that scale.
context.historical: Ancient Israel needed a clear system for dealing with the reality that people sin without always realizing it at the time; this chapter provided exactly that remedy, distinct from the willful, defiant sins addressed with harsher penalties elsewhere in the law.
context.canonical: The sin offering's careful distinctions by rank and its blood ritual become the vocabulary the book of Hebrews draws on most directly to explain both the inadequacy of animal sacrifice and the sufficiency of Christ's own blood.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide Israel with atonement for unintentional sin, graduated according to the sinner's position and its impact on the community, so that no rank of Israelite society was left without a remedy for guilt incurred in ignorance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribes an actual, detailed sacrificial procedure for old covenant Israel; it is not a pattern of animal sacrifice for the church. Its abiding principle is that unintentional wrongdoing is genuine sin requiring atonement, not something to be waved away as inconsequential.

debates.question: Why does the priest's sin, and the sin of the whole congregation, require the same costly bull, while a leader's requires only a goat and a common person's a female goat or lamb?

views.label: Greater office brings greater responsibility
views.case: The anointed priest represents the entire nation before God, so his personal sin corrupts the nation's standing before God, requiring the same weighty remedy as the sin of the whole congregation.

views.label: The scale reflects proximity to the sanctuary and its holiness
views.case: Others note that the priest's blood is brought further into the sanctuary, before the veil, than the leader's or common person's, whose blood only reaches the altar of burnt offering, suggesting the scale tracks contact with holy space as much as social rank.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that greater access to and responsibility for God's holy things brings greater accountability, a principle that runs throughout Scripture, compare James 3:1.

study.originalLanguages: Shegagah, unintentional sin, still requires chattat, a sin offering whose name shares the very root of the word for sin, showing that ignorance does not erase guilt. Each case closes with kaphar, atonement, resulting in the sinner being nasla, forgiven, a term describing guilt genuinely lifted away rather than merely postponed.

typology.type: The bull's blood brought into the sanctuary, its body burned outside the camp
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who suffered outside the city gate to sanctify His people through His own blood, Hebrews 13:11-12.

typology.type: Atonement graduated by rank, yet available to every level of society
typology.fulfillment: The gospel that reaches every rank, from leaders to common people, offering the same complete forgiveness through Christ, Galatians 3:28.

study.christ: Hebrews 13:11-12 draws a direct line from this chapter's pattern, bodies burned outside the camp while blood is brought into the sanctuary, to Jesus, who suffered outside the gate to sanctify the people through His own blood. Where these sacrifices had to be repeated for every unintentional sin discovered, Christ's single sacrifice actually cleanses the conscience once for all, Hebrews 9:14, rather than merely covering sin until the next offering was required.
study.god: God holds every level of society accountable for sin, even sin committed without deliberate intent, showing that ignorance never fully excuses wrongdoing before a holy God. Yet He provides a complete remedy, kaphar and full forgiveness, for every rank, from the highest priest to the humblest Israelite, revealing a justice matched every step by mercy.

crossReferences.note: Christ suffered outside the city gate to sanctify His people through His own blood, echoing the sin offering burned outside the camp.
If we confess our sins, God is faithful and just to forgive us and cleanse us from all unrighteousness.
David prays for cleansing from hidden faults, sins committed without full awareness, much like the unintentional sins addressed here.

study.oneStory: The sin offering shows that even unintentional wrongdoing creates real guilt before a holy God, and that God graciously provided atonement for every rank of Israelite society, from the anointed priest to the common person. This whole system anticipates Christ, whose blood, unlike these repeated animal sacrifices, actually and permanently cleanses the conscience of sin, even sins we do not fully recognize in ourselves.

quiz.q: What kind of sin does this chapter's sin offering specifically address?

options.text: Sin committed unintentionally, without deliberate intent to rebel.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly frames the whole chapter around unintentional sin, shegagah.

options.text: Only sins committed in open, deliberate defiance of God.
options.note: The text specifies unintentional sin as its focus, not deliberate rebellion.

options.text: Sins committed only by foreigners living among Israel.
options.note: The chapter addresses priest, congregation, leader, and common Israelite, not foreigners specifically.

options.text: No particular category of sin is in view.
options.note: Verse 2 specifically names unintentional sin as the subject.

quiz.q: What must the anointed priest bring as a sin offering if he sins and brings guilt on the people?

options.text: A young bull without blemish.
options.note: Verse 3 specifies this exact offering for the anointed priest.

options.text: Two turtledoves.
options.note: Birds are the provision for the poor in the guilt offering context, not the required offering here for the priest.

options.text: A female lamb.
options.note: That offering belongs to the ordinary Israelite's case, verse 32, not the anointed priest's.

options.text: No offering is required of the priest.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly requires a bull from the anointed priest.

quiz.q: Who lays their hands on the bull's head when the whole congregation of Israel sins unintentionally?

options.text: The elders of the congregation.
options.note: Verse 15 specifies the elders performing this act on behalf of the whole assembly.

options.text: Every individual Israelite personally.
options.note: The elders act representatively; not every individual is described as laying hands on the animal.

options.text: The anointed priest alone.
options.note: The priest handles the blood and offering afterward, but the elders lay hands on the bull's head.

options.text: No one lays hands on the offering in this case.
options.note: Verse 15 explicitly describes the elders performing this act.

quiz.q: What offering must a leader, as distinct from a common person, bring for unintentional sin?

options.text: An unblemished male goat.
options.note: Verse 23 specifies a male goat for the leader's sin offering.

options.text: A young bull, the same as the priest.
options.note: The bull is required specifically of the anointed priest and the whole congregation, not the leader.

options.text: A female goat or lamb, the same as a common person.
options.note: Verses 28 and 32 specify female animals for the common person; the leader's offering, verse 23, is a male goat.

options.text: No offering is specified for a leader.
options.note: Verse 23 gives a specific offering for the leader.

quiz.q: What is the consistent outcome stated at the end of each case in this chapter?

options.text: The priest makes atonement for the sinner, and he or they are forgiven.
options.note: This phrase, or a close variant, closes every case, verses 20, 26, 31, and 35.

options.text: The sinner is permanently barred from the camp.
options.note: The chapter describes atonement and forgiveness as the outcome, not permanent exclusion.

options.text: The sinner must repeat the offering seven times.
options.note: The sevenfold sprinkling applies to the blood ritual for the priest and congregation, not a sevenfold repetition of the whole offering.

options.text: No resolution or outcome is given.
options.note: Each case explicitly closes with atonement and forgiveness.

questions: This chapter takes unintentional sin seriously, sin you may not even realize you have committed. Ask God this week to search your heart for blind spots you have not noticed.

The offering required grew or shrank according to the sinner's position and responsibility. Where has God given you influence or responsibility, and are you taking the weight of that seriously before Him?
Every single case in this chapter ends the same way: atonement made, and the sinner forgiven. Do you actually believe you are forgiven, or do you still carry guilt Christ has already dealt with?

The whole congregation could sin together, unnoticed, until someone recognized it. Where might your community, church, or family be collectively blind to a sin that needs to be named and brought to God?

Christ's blood was brought into the true sanctuary once for all, while His body was broken outside the camp. How does knowing His sacrifice actually cleanses your conscience, not merely covers your sin, change how you deal with guilt today?

translationNotes.note: Unintentionally translates shegagah, describing an error or unwitting act; it is contrasted elsewhere in the Torah with sin committed defiantly, or with a high hand, Numbers 15:30, which received no such sacrificial remedy.
Bringing guilt on the people reflects the priest's representative role; his personal failure was understood to affect the whole nation's standing before God, since he ministered on their behalf.

Outside the camp to a ceremonially clean place where the ashes are poured out is the same location where the bodies of these sin offerings were burned, a detail Hebrews 13:11-12 applies directly to Christ's crucifixion outside Jerusalem's gate.
